angle_summary: Arnos Bistrot has built one of the strongest review reputations in Westport hospitality, and there is a clear opportunity to make that credibility work harder through targeted local SEO improvements and better on-site surfacing of trust signals. A focused, fixed-scope engagement addressing schema markup, mobile performance, and Google Reviews embedding would help convert existing organic visibility into more direct bookings.
proof_points: Mobile PageSpeed score of 65 versus a desktop score of 98 indicates a meaningful performance gap at the point where tourist and walk-in visitors are most likely to be searching.
A 4.9-star Google rating across 293 reviews is an exceptional trust signal that is currently displayed only through manually maintained testimonials rather than dynamically embedded verified reviews.
No blog or updates section is present, meaning the site publishes no fresh content to support local search queries around seasonal menus, events, or Westport dining intent.
recommended_offer_type: Local SEO starter (schema, pages, content plan)
offer_scope_points: Add Restaurant and LocalBusiness schema markup to the homepage covering location, opening hours, and review aggregate to improve local search result appearance.
Embed a dynamic Google Reviews widget to automatically surface the 4.9-star rating and recent reviews on-site, replacing the current manual testimonials display.
Deliver a lightweight local content plan outlining four to six post topics per quarter targeting Westport and Mayo dining queries, with recommended page structure and on-page copy guidance.
personalisation_notes: Arnos Bistrot holds a 4.9-star rating across 293 Google reviews, which is a standout signal in the Westport hospitality market and a natural hook for outreach framing.

The business is located in Westport, Co. Mayo, a tourist destination where mobile search intent from visitors is a commercially significant factor, making the mobile speed gap directly relevant to booking volume.
suggested_cta: I would be happy to send over a one-page summary of the specific changes and what they could mean for bookings — or if easier, we could walk through it on a short call whenever suits you this week or next.
avoid_saying: Avoid implying the current site is underperforming or letting the business down, as the owners are likely proud of their strong reputation and may be satisfied with existing booking levels.

Avoid suggesting they need to commit to ongoing blog writing or content management, as time constraints during peak season are a likely objection and the content plan should be framed as structured and low-effort.
unknowns_to_confirm: Whether the booking system is embedded directly on the site or redirects to a third-party platform such as OpenTable or ResDiary, as this affects how the mobile booking pathway and conversion framing should be positioned.
Whether any schema markup is currently implemented on the site, as this has been flagged as a likely gap but has not been confirmed as a definite absence.
